content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
May 4, 199 0
Brian W. l,laas
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA 94L2O
Re: Your Request for Informal
Ass istance
Our r.ile No. I -9 0-12 2
Dear Mr. l,laas :
You have requested informal, advice . concerning the Iobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1
OUESTION
Is an employee who spends less than 50 percent of his or her
time atternpting to influence Iegislative or admi,nistrative action
exempt from the definition of a lobbyist as an employee who has
been engaged rrprimarily to perforrn services other than influencing
legislative or administrative action?',
CONCI,USTON
Without specific facts concerning the duties, salary received
for lobbying activities in a calendar month, and anount of time
spent by the employee on influencing or atternpting to influence
legislative or adrninistrative action (lobbying activities), 
a
clear conclusion cannot be made. An employee who spends less than
50 percent of his or her time on lobbying activities 
roay still
qualify as a lobbyist.
1 
Gor".rr.ent Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A1l- statutory
references are to the Governrrent Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et ses. A1I references to regulations are to Tit1e
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advi.ce.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Bt'rx 8O7 . Sacrarncnto CA 958o4-tl8tr7 o (9 l6 ) 322-5660


Brian w. Maas
Page T$ro
ANALYSIS
Section 82039 defines a ttlobbyistrr as:
...any individual who is employed or contracts for
economic consideration .... to comrnunicate directly
or through his or her agents trith any elective stale
official, 
agency official 
or legislative official
for the purpose of influencing legislative or admj.n-
istrative action, if a substantial or reqular portion
of the activities 
for which he or she receives con-
sideration is for the purpose of influencing legis-
lative or adni-nistrative action.
Regulation 18239 establishes two tests for determining when
an individual,s activities in connection with influencing -
legislative or administrative action are ttsubstantiat or regular.rr
The tests are commonly referred to as the rtcontactsr test and the
xcompensationrr test. 
The rrcontactsi' test provides that a lobbyist
is an individuaL who:
...receives or becomes entitled to receive any amount of
compensation for engaging in direct comnunication, other than
administrative testimony, with gualifying officials 
for the
purpose of influencing legislative or adninistrative action
on at Least 25 separate occasions in any two consecutive
calendar months.
ReguLation 18239 (c) .
The rrcompensationrt test provides that an i.ndividual vho
engages. in any anount of direct conrnunication may also gualify as
a lobbyist if he or she:
.,.receives or becones entitled to receive at least $2,OOO in
compensation in any calendar rnonth for influencing
legislative or adninistrative action.
Regulation 18239 (b) .
However, Regulation 18239(b) excludes from the rcompensationr test
a trfull time employee engaged primarily to perform services other
than influencing legislative or adrninistrative action, or for
administrative testinony. rt
There is nothing in the Act, regulations, or past advice that
provides a percentage for deterrnining when an ernployee has been
engaged ttprimarilytr to perform services other than lobbying. An
employee who regularly spends 50 percent or more of his or her
tirne on lobbying activities 
wouLd certainly not be excluded from


Brian W. Maas
Page Three
the definition of lobbyistrr by the exception in Regulation
18239(b). on the other hand, Commission regulations specificatly
exclude from disclosure conpensation paid by a lobbyist employer
to enployees other than lobbyists vrho spend less than 10 percent
of their compensated time on lobbying activities. 
Therefore, it
could be assumed that an individual who does not meet the
rrcontactsrr test and hrho spends Less than 10 percent of his or her
compensated time on lobbying activities 
r.rould be covered by the
exception.
Holrever, whether a particular individual who spends nore than
10 percent of his or her tiroe engaged in lobbying activities is
considered to be engaged ttprinarilyrr to perforn services other
than lobbying will depend on the speqific facts involved. 
One
factor to consider in making such a determination \^/ou1d be whether
the individual reqularlv spends more than 10 percent of his or her
time each month on lobbying activities, 
including direct
communication with qualifying officiats.
If you have any additional questions, please contact ne at
(9161 322-5662.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Do
S. Braaten-Moen
ca1 Reform ConsuLtant
Enc losure
e

Brian w. Maas
